title: Cloud Architecture Overview
content: Cloud architecture is how individual technologies are integrated to create cloud environments. It's the design of the components and subcomponents that make up the cloud, such as databases, software capabilities, and applications, which are engineered to leverage the power of cloud resources to solve business problems.
insight: Architecture is the blueprint that ensures your cloud resources work together harmoniously.

title: Global Infrastructure
content: Cloud providers operate global networks of data centers. To understand how to deploy applications, you must understand the hierarchy of this infrastructure: Regions, Availability Zones, and Edge Locations.
insight: The world is your data center, but you must choose your locations wisely.

title: Regions
content: A region is a physical location in the world where a cloud provider has multiple Availability Zones. Regions are geographically isolated from each other to ensure that a disaster in one region doesn't affect another.
insight: Select a region based on data sovereignty, latency to users, and service availability.

title: Availability Zones (AZs)
content: An Availability Zone (AZ) consists of one or more discrete data centers, each with redundant power, networking, and connectivity. They are located within a region and are connected via low-latency, high-bandwidth networking.
insight: Deploying across multiple AZs is the key to High Availability.

title: Multi-AZ Deployment
content: By deploying your application in at least two AZs within a region, you protect yourself against the failure of a single data center. If one AZ goes down, the other continues to serve traffic.
insight: High Availability (HA) starts with 'Multi-AZ'.

title: Edge Locations
content: Edge Locations are endpoints for cloud services that are used for caching content. They are typically located in major cities around the world to reduce latency for end-users.
insight: Edge locations bring your static content closer to your users' devices.

title: Content Delivery Network (CDN)
content: A CDN uses Edge Locations to cache and deliver content like images, videos, and scripts. This speeds up the website for users regardless of how far they are from the primary server.
insight: CDNs are the 'Express Lanes' of the internet.

title: Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)
content: A VPC is a private, isolated section of the cloud where you can launch resources in a virtual network that you define. You have complete control over your virtual networking environment.
insight: Your VPC is your own private data center in the cloud.

title: Public vs. Private Subnets
content: Subnets allow you to group resources within a VPC. Public subnets have a route to the internet; private subnets do not. Web servers usually go in public subnets, while databases stay in private ones.
insight: Isolation is the first step in cloud security.

title: Internet Gateway
content: An Internet Gateway (IGW) is a horizontally scaled, redundant, and highly available VPC component that allows communication between your VPC and the internet.
insight: Without an IGW, your VPC is an island.

title: NAT Gateway
content: A NAT (Network Address Translation) Gateway allows resources in a private subnet to connect to the internet (e.g., for software updates) but prevents the internet from initiating a connection with those resources.
insight: NAT Gateways provide one-way outbound internet access for security.

title: Elastic Load Balancing (ELB)
content: A Load Balancer automatically distributes incoming application traffic across multiple targets, such as EC2 instances, in multiple Availability Zones.
insight: Load balancers are the traffic cops of your architecture.

title: Application Load Balancer (ALB)
content: ALB functions at the Application Layer (Layer 7). It makes routing decisions based on the content of the request, such as URL paths or host headers.
insight: Use ALB for HTTP and HTTPS traffic that needs smart routing.

title: Network Load Balancer (NLB)
content: NLB functions at the Transport Layer (Layer 4). It is capable of handling millions of requests per second while maintaining ultra-low latencies.
insight: Use NLB for high-performance TCP/UDP traffic.

title: Auto Scaling Groups (ASG)
content: An Auto Scaling Group contains a collection of EC2 instances that are treated as a logical grouping for the purposes of automatic scaling and management.
insight: ASG ensures you have the right number of instances to handle the load.

title: Dynamic Scaling
content: Dynamic scaling policies adjust the number of instances in your ASG based on live metrics like CPU utilization or request count per target.
insight: Only pay for the servers you need right now.

title: Predictive Scaling
content: Predictive scaling uses machine learning to schedule the right number of EC2 instances based on predicted demand patterns from your historical data.
insight: Scaling that happens before the traffic spike arrives.

title: Stateless Architecture
content: In a stateless architecture, servers do not store client data from previous requests. This allows any server to handle any incoming request, making horizontal scaling much easier.
insight: Store session data in a database or cache, not on the local disk.

title: Decoupling with Queues
content: Message queues allow different parts of an application to communicate asynchronously. If the processing part of your app is slow, the queue holds the messages until it can catch up.
insight: Queues act as buffers that prevent one service from overwhelming another.

title: Event-Driven Architecture
content: An event-driven architecture uses events to trigger and communicate between decoupled services. It's common in modern microservices applications using services like Lambda or SQS.
insight: Architecture that reacts to changes in real-time.

title: Microservices
content: Microservices break an application into small, independent services that communicate over APIs. This allows teams to develop, deploy, and scale services independently.
insight: Small services are easier to manage and faster to deploy.

title: Vertical Database Scaling
content: Vertical scaling (scaling up) involves adding more CPU or RAM to your database instance. This is the simplest way to handle more load but has a physical limit.
insight: Scaling up is the 'bigger hammer' approach.

title: Read Replicas
content: Read Replicas allow you to offload read traffic from your primary database to one or more replicas. This improves performance for read-heavy applications.
insight: Scale your reads by creating copies of your data.

title: Database Sharding
content: Sharding involves splitting a large database into smaller, faster, more easily managed parts called data shards. Each shard is stored on a separate database server instance.
insight: Sharding is the ultimate way to scale out massive datasets.

title: Caching with Redis/Memcached
content: In-memory caches like Redis or Memcached store frequently accessed data. Fetching data from memory is significantly faster than fetching it from a disk-based database.
insight: Cache early and cache often to reduce database load.

title: Route 53 and Global DNS
content: Managed DNS services like Route 53 allow you to route users to the healthiest and closest resources globally using various routing policies.
insight: DNS is the 'entry point' to your global architecture.

title: Latency-Based Routing
content: Latency-based routing directs user requests to the AWS region that provides the lowest latency for that specific user.
insight: Keep your users happy by minimizing the distance data travels.

title: Failover Routing
content: Failover routing allows you to configure an active-passive setup. If your primary resource is unhealthy, DNS automatically points users to your standby resource.
insight: Automatic DNS-level disaster recovery.

title: Security Groups
content: Security Groups act as a virtual firewall for your instances to control inbound and outbound traffic. They operate at the instance level, not the subnet level.
insight: Security groups are your first line of defense at the server level.

title: Network ACLs
content: Network Access Control Lists (NACLs) provide an optional layer of security for your VPC that acts as a firewall for controlling traffic in and out of one or more subnets.
insight: NACLs provide subnet-level security and are stateless.

title: WAF
content: A Web Application Firewall (WAF) helps protect your web applications from common web exploits that could affect application availability or compromise security (e.g., SQL injection, XSS).
insight: WAF filters out malicious traffic before it reaches your app.

title: DDoS Protection (AWS Shield)
content: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) protection services automatically detect and mitigate attacks designed to overwhelm your application with traffic.
insight: Don't let bad actors drown your application in traffic.

title: IaC Benefits
content: Infrastructure as Code allows you to define your architecture in files. This ensures your dev, test, and prod environments are identical and reduces human error.
insight: If it's not in code, it's a liability.

title: Cloud-Native Design
content: Cloud-native design focuses on building applications that specifically leverage cloud features like auto-scaling, managed services, and serverless.
insight: Don't just move to the cloud; build FOR the cloud.

title: The 12-Factor App
content: The 12-factor app is a methodology for building SaaS applications that are optimized for modern cloud deployments, emphasizing portability and scalability.
insight: A gold standard for building modern, cloud-ready applications.

title: CloudWatch and Metrics
content: Monitoring services provide data and actionable insights to monitor your applications, respond to system-wide performance changes, and optimize resource utilization.
insight: You can't manage what you don't measure.

title: Centralized Logging
content: Centralized logging collects logs from all your instances and services into a single searchable location, which is crucial for troubleshooting in distributed systems.
insight: One place to look when things go wrong across 100 servers.

title: Distributed Tracing (AWS X-Ray)
content: Distributed tracing helps developers analyze and debug distributed applications, such as those built using a microservices architecture.
insight: Follow a request as it travels through multiple services.

title: Trade-offs in Architecture
content: Every architectural decision involves trade-offs between cost, performance, security, and complexity. There is no 'perfect' architecture, only the right one for your specific needs.
insight: Architecting is the art of choosing the right trade-offs.

title: Conclusion
content: Modern cloud architecture is about more than just servers; it's about building resilient, scalable, and automated systems that can adapt to changing demands instantly.
insight: The cloud architect's goal is to make the infrastructure invisible to the developer.
